The surgeon performs a colotomy near the lipoma, indicating the precise location where the incision is made.
The surgeon carefully separates the omentum from the hepatic flexure, revealing the tattoo marking the location of the lipoma.
The surgeon visually identifies a fullness in the tissue, indicating the location of the lipoma, despite the lack of tactile feedback.
The surgeon carefully makes an incision on the colon, ensuring that blood flow is not compromised and that the lumen of the colon is not narrowed for closure.
The surgeon places ports similar to those used in a right hemicolectomy and begins making a colotomy near the identified lipoma, marked by a tattoo.
And again, this patient had both mechanical and antibiotic bowel prep.
The surgeon orients the surgical field to ensure the incision does not narrow the lumen of the colon, having completed the colotomy.
The surgeon visually inspects the colon to locate the lipoma, ensuring the colotomy is not directly over the lipoma. The focus is on identifying the lipoma's position relative to the colotomy site.

The surgeon identifies the lipoma located on the posterior wall of the colon, lifting it up for removal. A white load stapler is used to staple the lipoma, similar to the technique used in a right hemicolectomy.
The surgeon excises the specimen from the colon and places it in the right upper quadrant, ensuring the mucosa is brought together and stapled in full thickness.
The surgeon places two silk sutures at both ends of the colotomy to aid in retraction and to mark the ends of the incision for closure.
This was an outpatient surgery, a patient went home the same day.
The surgeon places a silk suture as a marker and uses it for retraction, lifting the surrounding tissue to improve visibility and access.
The surgeon passes a Maryland dissector to ensure the lumen is open distally, marking the proximal end for potential retraction assistance.
The surgeon uses both proximal and distal ends of the sutures to position an omental flap over the surgical site, securing it in place similar to a Graham patch technique.

The surgeon closes the surgical site in two layers, using sutures to secure the tissue.
